content:
2 min read
Just now

--

One of the first things I noticed early in my career had nothing to do with technology.
By the time developers reached their mid-thirties, the fastest among them were already spending more time in meeting rooms than writing code. Others followed a few years later. Nobody explicitly told them to stop developing. It simply happened. Little by little, code gave way to coordination.
I never wanted to become a manager.
I went to graduate school and studied Management Information Systems because I wanted to communicate better with customers and become a better engineer. It was never a stepping stone toward management. More than money or title, I wanted something much simpler: to keep writing code as I got older.
That was one of the reasons I left full-time employment and became a freelance developer.
I was fortunate. I spent more than a decade building software for the same client. In many ways, I achieved what I had hoped for. I remained an engineer for much longer than most people around me.
But over time, the work began to change.
There were more coordination meetings, more discussions across teams, more time spent aligning priorities and resolving organizational issues. Gradually, I found myself investing more energy in people than in software.
Eventually, I left.
That was when I truly entered the world of freelancing.
What surprised me was not the uncertainty. It was how difficult it was to find the kind of work I had imagined.
A place where an experienced developer is simply expected to develop.
Those roles are surprisingly rare.
Large systems require large projects. Large projects require experienced engineers. Yet the more experienced you become, the more likely you are to be pulled into planning, coordination, leadership, and management. Looking around, I realized this wasn’t unique to one company. As careers progress, meetings replace coding. Coordination replaces design.
For a while, I wondered if the problem was me.
Maybe I simply wasn’t good enough. Maybe truly exceptional engineers get to spend their entire careers writing code.
But the more I observed, the less I believed that.
This wasn’t about individual ability.
It was about the structure itself.
Organizations are designed in a way that gradually moves senior engineers away from development. Writing code becomes something you mentor others to do, rather than something you are expected to keep doing yourself.
I still don’t know whether this is uniquely Korean or simply the natural trajectory of our profession.
What I do know is this.
In a world where it is increasingly difficult for experienced developers to simply remain developers, wanting to spend your career writing code is no longer a modest dream.
It’s a surprisingly ambitious one.
